name: Baldwin, Carryl L.
recent_1: Abubshait, A., Pak, R., Rogers, W. A., Šabanović, S., Lighthall, N., & Baldwin, C. L. (2025). Robotics and AI: Challenges and Opportunities for Aging Populations. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69(1), 692-694. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251357940
recent_2: Mehta, R. K., Tyagi, A., Kim, J. H., Yu, X., & Baldwin, C. L. (2023). 25 years of Neuroergonomics: Will we get to the Golden Jubilee? Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 67(1), 18-20. DOI: 10.1177/21695067231192274
recent_3: Greenwood, P. M., & Baldwin, C. L. (2022). Preferred Sources of Information, Knowledge, and Acceptance of Automated Vehicle Systems: Effects of Gender and Age. Frontiers in Psychology, 13, 806552. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2022.806552
recent_1_synopsis: A panel/proceedings contribution examining how robotics and AI technologies can support aging populations, addressing both the design challenges and opportunities for older adults. It reflects Baldwin's role bridging human factors, aging, and emerging autonomous/AI systems within her Regional Institute on Aging work at Wichita State. The piece situates human-automation interaction within the context of older users' needs and acceptance.
recent_2_synopsis: A retrospective and forward-looking proceedings piece reflecting on 25 years of the neuroergonomics field and its trajectory toward a future 'golden jubilee.' Co-authored with neuroergonomics leaders, it discusses the maturation of brain-based measures of operator state, workload, and performance. The contribution underscores Baldwin's standing as a senior voice in neuroergonomics.
recent_3_synopsis: A Frontiers in Psychology study examining how gender and age shape where people prefer to obtain information about automated vehicle systems, their knowledge of those systems, and their acceptance of them. The work informs the design of education and outreach for automated driving, especially for older drivers. It extends Baldwin's program on driver behavior, aging, and human-automation interaction in transportation.
seminal_1: May, J. F., & Baldwin, C. L. (2009). Driver fatigue: The importance of identifying causal factors of fatigue when considering detection and countermeasure technologies. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 12(3), 218-224. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2008.11.005
seminal_2: Baldwin, C. L., Roberts, D. M., Barragan, D., Lee, J. D., Lerner, N., & Higgins, J. S. (2017). Detecting and Quantifying Mind Wandering during Simulated Driving. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 11, 406. DOI: 10.3389/fnhum.2017.00406
seminal_1_synopsis: A foundational and highly cited (approximately 670 citations per Google Scholar, June 2026) conceptual paper distinguishing sleep-related, passive (task-underload), and active (task-overload) forms of driver fatigue, and arguing that fatigue countermeasures and detection technologies must be matched to the causal type. It is foundational because it provided a widely adopted taxonomy of fatigue that reframed how the field thinks about driver-state detection and intervention. The active/passive/sleep-related distinction has shaped subsequent fatigue and vigilance research.
seminal_2_synopsis: A widely cited (approximately 280 citations per Google Scholar, June 2026) empirical study demonstrating that mind wandering during simulated driving can be detected and quantified using behavioral and psychophysiological (including EEG/eye) measures. It is influential as one of the first rigorous attempts to operationally measure internally-driven driver distraction, a construct distinct from external visual distraction. The work has anchored later research on attention lapses and driver state monitoring. (Note: the book 'Auditory Cognition and Human Performance,' CRC Press 2012, approx. 133 citations, is arguably also seminal but is a monograph and lacks a Crossref journal DOI; it is omitted here in favor of two DOI-verified journal articles.)
expertise_overview: Baldwin's research centers on applied auditory cognition and the design of auditory and multimodal displays, including in-vehicle collision warnings, navigation/routing cues, and alarms. A second major thread is mental workload and operator-state assessment, drawing on neuroergonomics methods such as EEG, eye tracking, and other psychophysiological measures. Her transportation human factors work spans driver fatigue, vigilance, mind wandering and distraction, and human-automation/automated-vehicle interaction. A strong cognitive-aging dimension runs through her work, examining age-related changes in speech processing, attention, and the design of transportation and AI/robotic systems for older adults. Methodologically she combines driving simulation, laboratory psychophysics, neuroergonomic measurement, and survey/acceptance studies. Domains include surface transportation, automation, aging, and operator monitoring in vigilance-demanding tasks.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Author confirmed as Carryl L. Baldwin, Carl and Rozina Cassat Distinguished Professor of Aging and Director of the Regional Institute on Aging, Human Factors Psychology Program, Wichita State University (previously George Mason University, Human Factors and Applied Cognition). Sources: Wichita State faculty page (wichita.edu profile), Google Scholar (user TyqbsCoAAAAJ), ResearchGate, ScienceDirect author profile, Crossref. Stated interests match seed: auditory cognition, neuroergonomics, in-vehicle warnings/alerts, mental workload, driver/pilot behavior, aging, human-automation interaction; author of the book 'Auditory Cognition and Human Performance' (CRC Press, 2012). All six included DOIs were verified to resolve via Crossref (api.crossref.org) with matching titles and Baldwin among the authors. The four pre-anchored Human Factors journal DOIs were also re-verified and resolve. Citation counts (driver fatigue approx. 670; mind wandering approx. 280; book approx. 133) are from Google Scholar profile as of June 17, 2026, and are approximate Scholar figures rather than Crossref/Web of Science counts. No DOIs were invented. The 2012 book is a likely-seminal item but lacks a Crossref journal DOI and is flagged accordingly. recent_3 is dated 2022 (just outside the preferred 2023-2026 window) but was included as a peer-reviewed journal article; both more recent items (2025, 2023) are HFES proceedings.
